title: Dyeing leather upholstery
subtitle: Degrease, apply alcohol dye in thin coats, and seal
excerpt: How to recolour or change the colour of pigmented or semi-aniline leather upholstery using alcohol-based leather dye: preparation, degreasing, applying thin coats, and finishing with a sealant.
sourceNotes: Leather Conservation Centre technical notes. Fiebings and Angelus product guides (publicly available). British Leather Confederation guidance.

content.text: Leather dye gives a more natural, permanent result than a surface paint coat.
Alcohol-based dyes

penetrate the leather fibre and cannot be wiped off once set. Preparation is the critical step: dye applied to a surface still carrying factory finish or grease will not bond and will peel. Work in a well-ventilated space; alcohol solvents are flammable and produce vapour during application.
Is your leather dyeable?

Aniline and semi-aniline leathers take dye most readily. Heavily coated pigmented leathers require stripping of the surface coating first. Patent leather and leatherette (synthetic PU) cannot be dyed with alcohol dyes. Test the surface absorbency with a drop of water: if it absorbs, the leather will take dye well.
Step 1, clean and degrease
Clean the entire surface with a pH-neutral leather cleaner. Allow to dry fully. Apply a
leather preparer and deglazer

with a lint-free cloth using firm circular motions: this removes factory finish and body oils from the leather surface. The cloth will pick up colour even if the leather looks clean; continue until the cloth comes away with no more residue. Allow to dry for 30 minutes.
Step 2, abrade pigmented surfaces

For pigmented leather, lightly abrade the surface with 180-grit sandpaper or 000 steel wool to remove the final gloss layer. Wipe off the dust with a dry cloth. This step is not needed for aniline or semi-aniline leather.
Step 3, apply the dye in thin coats

Apply the leather dye with a small piece of lint-free cloth folded into a pad, or with a soft brush for tight areas. Work in thin, even coats using circular strokes. Each coat dries in 5 to 10 minutes. Apply three to five coats, allowing each to dry before the next. Thin coats give an even, penetrating result; a single thick coat leaves brush marks and dries uneven.
Step 4, seal the surface

Once the final coat of dye has dried fully (allow at least 2 hours), apply a leather finisher or sealant to lock in the colour and restore a protective surface layer. Choose a matte, satin, or gloss finish to match the original leather. Apply in two thin coats, buffing lightly between coats with a dry cloth.
Common mistakes
Skipping the degreasing step.
Dye applied over residual grease or factory finish will peel within days.
Applying too few coats.
Two coats rarely give even coverage, especially when going from light to dark. Three coats minimum; five for pale-to-dark changes.
Not testing on a hidden area first.
Dye penetration and final colour depend on the leather type; test on the underside of a cushion or inside a seat corner before committing to the visible surfaces.
